__comment__: This file was automatically created by mcresources
name: Soil Fertility

pages.text: There are eight varieties of soil occurring in different locations throughout the world. These soils range from very fertile, providing a 20% boost to all $(l:mechanics/fertilizers)fertilizers$() applied to it, and to all nutrients recharged by $(l:mechanics/crops)crops$(), to very poor, providing 20% less than typical soil.
pages.title: Soil Fertility

pages.text: Soil quality only impacts the effectiveness of fertilizers; unfertilized crops grow at the same rate regardless of what soil they are planted in. While nutrients can be consumed by crops, soil quality is not degraded by subsequent plantings.$(br)$(br)The following pages outline each soil type.

pages.name: Entisol
pages.text: Entisol is the most common soil type, found throughout the world. It is a normal quality soil, receiving no bonus nutrients.

pages.name: Andisol
pages.text: Andisol is found in volcanic regions. It is a high quality soil, receiving 10% bonus nutrients.

pages.name: Fluvisol
pages.text: Fluvisol is found along rivers in regions with wet and dry seasons. It is a high quality soil, receiving 10% bonus nutrients.

pages.name: Alfisol
pages.text: Alfisol is found in temperate broadleaf forests. It is a high quality soil, receiving 10% bonus nutrients.

pages.name: Mollisol
pages.text: Mollisol is found in cold grasslands. It is a very high quality soil, receiving 20% bonus nutrients.

pages.name: Podzol
pages.text: Podzol is found in conifer forests. It is a poor quality soil, receiving 10% less nutrients.

pages.name: Aridisol
pages.text: Aridisol is found in dry regions. It is a poor quality soil, receiving 10% less nutrients.

pages.name: Oxisol
pages.text: Oxisol is found in wet tropical regions. It is a very poor quality soil, receiving 20% less nutrients.
